section.title: Oracles against Ammon, Edom, and the East
section.sectionRationale: This chapter gathers five shorter oracles: Ammon, who had seized Israelite land; Edom, Israel's brother nation through Esau; Damascus, the old Aramean capital; Kedar and Hazor, the tent dwelling peoples of the eastern desert; and Elam, far to the east near Persia. Each nation is judged for its own particular pride or violence, and three of the five close with an unlooked-for promise of restoration.
section.thesis: The LORD's justice reaches every neighbor of Judah, near or far, kin or stranger, tent dweller or walled city; and even here His severity is threaded through with promises that He will one day restore what He has broken.
section.frameNote: Five movements, one nation each: Ammon, who seized Gad's land; Edom, proud in the clefts of the rocks; Damascus, agitated like the sea; Kedar and Hazor, a people dwelling alone; and Elam, whose bow will be shattered yet whose captivity will be turned in the last days.

verses.text: Concerning the Ammonites, this is what the LORD says: “Has Israel no sons? Is he without heir? Why then has {{milcom|Milcom}} taken possession of Gad? Why have his people settled in their cities?

Therefore, behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will sound the battle cry against Rabbah of the Ammonites. It will become a heap of ruins, and its villages will be burned. Then Israel will drive out their dispossessors, says the LORD.

Wail, O Heshbon, for Ai has been destroyed; cry out, O daughters of Rabbah! Put on sackcloth and mourn; run back and forth within your walls, for Milcom will go into exile together with his priests and officials.
Why do you boast of your valleys, your valleys so fruitful, O faithless daughter? You trust in your riches and say, ‘Who can come against me?’
Behold, I am about to bring terror upon you, declares the Lord GOD of Hosts, from all those around you. You will each be driven headlong, with no one to regather the fugitives.
Yet afterward I will {{shuvshevut|restore}} the Ammonites from captivity,” declares the LORD.

units.label: Concerning Edom
units.note: wisdom failed, pride in the rocks answered

verses.text: Concerning Edom, this is what the LORD of Hosts says: “Is there no longer wisdom in Teman? Has counsel perished from the prudent? Has their wisdom decayed?
Turn and run! Lie low, O dwellers of Dedan, for I will bring disaster on Esau at the time I punish him.
If grape gatherers came to you, would they not leave some gleanings? Were thieves to come in the night, would they not steal only what they wanted?

But I will strip Esau bare; I will uncover his hiding places, and he will be unable to conceal himself. His descendants will be destroyed along with his relatives and neighbors, and he will be no more.
Abandon your orphans; I will preserve their lives. Let your widows trust in Me.”
For this is what the LORD says: “If those who do not deserve to drink the cup must drink it, can you possibly remain unpunished? You will not go unpunished, for you must drink it too.
For by Myself I have sworn, declares the LORD, that Bozrah will become a desolation, a disgrace, a ruin, and a curse, and all her cities will be in ruins forever.”
I have heard a message from the LORD; an envoy has been sent to the nations: “Assemble yourselves to march against her! Rise up for battle!”
“For behold, I will make you small among nations, despised among men.

The terror you cause and the pride of your heart have deceived you, O dwellers in the clefts of the rocks, O occupiers of the mountain summit. Though you elevate your nest like the eagle, even from there I will bring you down,” declares the LORD.
“Edom will become an object of horror. All who pass by will be appalled and will scoff at all her wounds.
As Sodom and Gomorrah were overthrown along with their neighbors,” says the LORD, “no one will dwell there; no man will abide there.

Behold, one will come up like a lion from the thickets of the Jordan to the watered pasture. For in an instant I will chase Edom from her land. Who is the chosen one I will appoint for this? For who is like Me, and who can challenge Me? What shepherd can stand against Me?”

Therefore hear the plans that the LORD has drawn up against Edom and the strategies He has devised against the people of Teman: Surely the little ones of the flock will be dragged away; certainly their pasture will be made desolate because of them.
At the sound of their fall the earth will quake; their cry will resound to the Red Sea.
Look! An eagle will soar and swoop down, spreading its wings over Bozrah. In that day the hearts of Edom’s mighty men will be like the heart of a woman in labor.
Concerning Damascus: “Hamath and Arpad are put to shame, for they have heard a bad report; they are agitated like the sea; their anxiety cannot be calmed.
Damascus has become feeble; she has turned to flee. Panic has gripped her; anguish and pain have seized her like a woman in labor.
How is the city of praise not forsaken, the town that brings Me joy?
For her young men will fall in the streets, and all her warriors will be silenced in that day,” declares the LORD of Hosts.
“I will set fire to the walls of Damascus; it will consume the fortresses of Ben-hadad.”

units.label: Concerning Kedar and Hazor
units.note: a people dwelling alone, without gates or bars

verses.text: Concerning Kedar and the kingdoms of Hazor, which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon defeated, this is what the LORD says: “Rise up, advance against Kedar, and destroy the people of the east!
They will take their tents and flocks, their tent curtains and all their goods. They will take their camels for themselves. They will shout to them: ‘Terror is on every side!’
Run! Escape quickly! Lie low, O residents of Hazor,” declares the LORD, “for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon has drawn up a plan against you; he has devised a strategy against you.
Rise up, advance against a nation at ease, one that dwells securely,” declares the LORD. “They have no gates or bars; they live alone.

Their camels will become plunder, and their large herds will be spoil. I will scatter to the wind in every direction those who shave their temples; I will bring calamity on them from all sides,” declares the LORD.
“Hazor will become a haunt for jackals, a desolation forever. No one will dwell there; no man will abide there.”

units.label: Concerning Elam
units.note: the bow shattered, yet restored at last

verses.text: This is the word of the LORD that came to Jeremiah the prophet concerning Elam at the beginning of the reign of Zedekiah king of Judah.
This is what the LORD of Hosts says: “Behold, I will shatter Elam’s bow, the mainstay of their might.
I will bring the four winds against Elam from the four corners of the heavens, and I will scatter them to all these winds. There will not be a nation to which Elam’s exiles will not go.

So I will shatter Elam before their foes, before those who seek their lives. I will bring disaster upon them, even My fierce anger,” declares the LORD. “I will send out the sword after them until I finish them off.
I will set My throne in Elam, and destroy its king and officials,” declares the LORD.
“Yet in the last days, I will restore Elam from captivity,” declares the LORD.

milcom.gloss: Milcom, the national god of the Ammonites, sometimes called Molech, associated elsewhere in Scripture with child sacrifice, 1 Kings 11:7. That Milcom goes into exile with his own priests, verse 3, shows him powerless to save the very people who served him.

shuvshevut.gloss: A Hebrew idiom meaning to turn or restore the captivity, that is, to reverse a nation's fortunes and bring its people home. It closes the oracles on Moab, 48:47, Ammon, 49:6, and Elam, 49:39, a small, recurring shadow of hope folded into even the harshest judgments.

basic.context: This chapter strings together five short oracles against Judah's remaining near neighbors: Ammon, who had seized Israelite territory in Gad; Edom, Israel's own kin through Esau, proud in her mountain strongholds; Damascus, the old Aramean capital; Kedar and Hazor, unwalled tent dwelling peoples of the eastern desert; and Elam, far to the east. Each is judged for its own particular offense, whether land grabbing, pride, or violence, yet three of the five, Ammon, Moab in the previous chapter, and Elam, close with a promise that the LORD will one day restore what He has broken.
basic.hermeneutics: These are real historical oracles against real ancient peoples, substantially fulfilled through the campaigns of Babylon and, in the case of Kedar and Hazor, explicitly tied to a Babylonian defeat, verse 28. They are not predictions about modern nations bearing similar names. The abiding truth is that the LORD's justice is not partial to Israel's enemies alone or to Israel's kin alone; He judges every nation by the same standard, and every nation, however judged, remains within reach of His mercy.
basic.originalLanguages: Milcom, Ammon's god, is named twice and shown powerless, exiled with his own priests, verse 3. Across three of these oracles the Hebrew idiom shuv shevut, to restore the captivity, appears as a settled formula of hope, verses 6 and 39, showing that even the LORD's severest sentences are not always His last word for a nation.
basic.typology: The recurring shuv shevut promise, restoration from captivity for humbled nations, anticipates the wider ingathering of the nations promised through Christ, in whom people from Ammon, Edom, and Elam's regions would one day be reconciled to God, Ephesians 2:11-13. Edom's proud eagle's nest brought down from the rocks, verse 16, anticipates every high thing that exalts itself against God being humbled before Christ, 2 Corinthians 10:5.
basic.christ: Across five different peoples, the LORD's justice and mercy hold together: judgment falls in full, yet Ammon and Elam are promised restoration, and Edom's orphans and widows are told, remarkably, 'I will preserve their lives, let your widows trust in Me,' verse 11, mercy spoken even into an oracle of near total destruction. This pattern, wrath poured out yet mercy still offered, is fulfilled at the cross, where Christ absorbed the full wrath the nations deserved so that from Ammon to Elam, from the ends of the earth, a people could be gathered into God's family, Acts 2:9 lists Elamites among those who heard the gospel at Pentecost.
basic.god: God is sovereign over every nation regardless of size, geography, or kinship to Israel: walled Damascus, unwalled Kedar, mountain bound Edom, and distant Elam all answer to the same LORD of Hosts. His anger against pride and violence is real and unsparing, yet His compassion reaches even further, holding out restoration to nations that, from a human point of view, had earned only destruction.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 19:36-38 and Genesis 25:1-4 trace Ammon and Moab's, and later Edom's, kinship to Israel through Lot and Esau. Obadiah gives an entire prophecy devoted to Edom's judgment for violence against Jacob. Acts 2:9 lists Elamites among the nations present at Pentecost, an early sign of the promised restoration reaching even that distant people. Ephesians 2:11-13 describes Gentiles once far off being brought near through Christ.
basic.oneStory: Five very different nations, near kin and distant strangers alike, are shown answering to the same LORD, and three of the five are promised restoration even after severe judgment. That widening circle of mercy toward the nations comes to its fullness in Christ, in whom people from Ammon to Elam, once far off, are brought near and gathered into the one family of God.

context.literary: Five distinct oracles gathered under one chapter heading, each following a similar shape: an indictment naming the nation's particular pride or offense, a sentence of destruction, and, in three cases, a closing word of future restoration echoing the same shuv shevut formula used for Moab in the previous chapter.
context.historical: Ammon and Moab, kin through Lot, and Edom, kin through Esau, were long-standing rivals of Israel east and south of the Jordan and Dead Sea. Damascus was the old Aramean capital, already broken by Assyria a century earlier but revived here as a symbol of any proud city. Kedar and Hazor represent unwalled, tent-dwelling Arab tribes of the eastern desert, explicitly said to have been struck by Nebuchadnezzar, verse 28. Elam lay far east, in what is now southwestern Iran, near the rising power of the Medes and Persians.
context.canonical: Edom in particular receives an entire prophetic book of judgment, Obadiah, for violence against Jacob at the time of Jerusalem's fall; Ammon and Moab appear together often, Amos 1:13-15, Zephaniah 2:8-11; and Elam's later inclusion among the nations gathered at Pentecost, Acts 2:9, shows the LORD's promised restoration of the nations already beginning to unfold in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To declare that the LORD's justice extends over every nation surrounding Judah, kin or stranger, near or far, and that His severity toward each is nonetheless interwoven with genuine promises of future mercy for several of them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: These passages describe specific historical judgments on specific ancient peoples; they do not prescribe hostility toward any nation or ethnicity today. The abiding principle is that pride, violence, and false confidence invite the LORD's judgment wherever they are found, and that His mercy is wider than any single nation's history of hostility toward His people.

debates.question: Why do Ammon and Elam receive an explicit promise of restoration while Damascus and Kedor and Hazor do not?

views.label: Not every oracle needs to repeat the same formula
views.case: The shuv shevut promise for Moab, Ammon, and Elam may simply reflect the prophetic collection's variety rather than a deliberate exclusion of Damascus or Kedar and Hazor from any future mercy; brevity does not necessarily mean permanent exclusion.

views.label: Edom's own promise of preserved life, verse 11, functions similarly
views.case: Even where the formal restoration formula is absent, as with Edom, a distinct promise appears, 'I will preserve their lives, let your widows trust in Me,' verse 11, showing mercy folded into judgment in a different shape for each nation, not only through the repeated formula.

debates.bearing: Either way, the chapter as a whole resists reading any nation's judgment as God's final word; mercy appears in some form even where destruction is most severe.

study.originalLanguages: Milcom, Ammon's god, is shown powerless, exiled with his own priests, verse 3. The recurring Hebrew idiom shuv shevut, to restore the captivity, verses 6 and 39, functions as a settled formula of hope across these oracles, alongside Moab's identical promise in the previous chapter, showing a deliberate pattern of mercy folded into judgment.

typology.type: The shuv shevut promise of restored captivity for Ammon and Elam
typology.fulfillment: The wider gathering of the nations to God through Christ, in whom those once far off are brought near, Ephesians 2:11-13.

typology.type: Edom's proud nest among the rocks brought down
typology.fulfillment: Every proud thing that exalts itself against the knowledge of God being humbled before Christ, 2 Corinthians 10:5.

study.christ: Across five different peoples, the LORD's justice and mercy hold together: judgment falls in full, yet Ammon and Elam are promised restoration, and Edom's orphans and widows are told, remarkably, 'I will preserve their lives, let your widows trust in Me,' verse 11, mercy spoken even into an oracle of near total destruction. This pattern, wrath poured out yet mercy still offered, is fulfilled at the cross, where Christ absorbed the full wrath the nations deserved so that from Ammon to Elam, from the ends of the earth, a people could be gathered into God's family; Acts 2:9 lists Elamites among those who heard the gospel at Pentecost.
study.god: God is sovereign over every nation regardless of size, geography, or kinship to Israel: walled Damascus, unwalled Kedar, mountain bound Edom, and distant Elam all answer to the same LORD of Hosts. His anger against pride and violence is real and unsparing, yet His compassion reaches even further, holding out restoration to nations that, from a human point of view, had earned only destruction.

crossReferences.note: Traces Ammon and Moab's descent from Lot, explaining their kinship with Israel.
An entire prophetic book devoted to Edom's judgment for violence against her brother Jacob.
Elamites are listed among the nations present at Pentecost, an early sign of the promised restoration reaching even that distant people.
Gentiles once far off are brought near through the blood of Christ, the fulfillment of the mercy hinted at in these oracles.

study.oneStory: Five very different nations, near kin and distant strangers alike, are shown answering to the same LORD, and three of the five are promised restoration even after severe judgment. That widening circle of mercy toward the nations comes to its fullness in Christ, in whom people from Ammon to Elam, once far off, are brought near and gathered into the one family of God.

quiz.q: Why is Ammon indicted in verse 1?

options.text: Because her god Milcom had taken possession of Gad, Israelite territory.
options.note: Verse 1 opens with exactly this charge, Ammon's seizure of Israel's land.

options.text: Because Ammon had refused to pay tribute to Babylon.
options.note: Not the charge given in this text; the indictment concerns Ammon's seizure of Gad.

options.text: Because Ammon had allied with Egypt against Judah.
options.note: Plausible in general terms of ancient politics, but this specific oracle names the seizure of Gad as the offense.

options.text: Because Ammon had never worshiped any god.
options.note: The opposite; Ammon's worship of Milcom is explicitly named throughout the passage.

quiz.q: What is Edom's core sin according to verse 16?

options.text: The terror she caused and the pride of her heart, trusting her mountain strongholds.
options.note: Verse 16 names this pride and false security directly, comparing Edom to an eagle nesting among the rocks.

options.text: Refusing to worship any god at all.
options.note: Not the charge in this passage; Edom's sin named here is pride and false security, not irreligion.

options.text: Failing to pay taxes to Judah.
options.note: No such charge appears in this text.

options.text: Building too many cities.
options.note: Not quite; the issue is not the number of cities but the pride and false security they gave Edom.

quiz.q: What distinguishes Kedar and Hazor from the other nations judged in this chapter, according to verse 31?

options.text: They have no gates or bars and live alone, an unwalled and undefended people.
options.note: Verse 31 specifically describes this vulnerability, unlike the walled cities of Damascus or Edom's mountain strongholds.

options.text: They are the wealthiest of all the nations named.
options.note: Their wealth in camels and herds is mentioned, verse 32, but their defining feature named in verse 31 is their lack of walls.

options.text: They alone worship the LORD.
options.note: No such claim is made about Kedar and Hazor in this text.

options.text: They alone are spared from judgment.
options.note: Not so; Kedar and Hazor are judged severely in this passage, becoming a haunt for jackals, verse 33.

quiz.q: What promise closes the oracle against Elam, verse 39?

options.text: The LORD will restore Elam from captivity in the last days.
options.note: Verse 39 explicitly promises this restoration after Elam's severe judgment.

options.text: Elam will conquer Babylon and rule the region.
options.note: No such promise appears; the hope offered is restoration, not conquest.

options.text: Elam will be destroyed forever with no future mentioned.
options.note: The opposite; verse 39 explicitly promises future restoration.

options.text: Elam's king will be spared from judgment.
options.note: Not accurate; verse 38 explicitly says the LORD will destroy Elam's king and officials before the restoration promise in verse 39.

quiz.q: What unites the mercy shown to Ammon, Edom, and Elam in this chapter?

options.text: Each nation, though severely judged, is given some word of preserved life or future restoration.
options.note: Ammon and Elam receive the shuv shevut restoration formula, and Edom is told the LORD will preserve her orphans and widows, verse 11.

options.text: None of the three nations actually experiences any real judgment.
options.note: The opposite; all three undergo severe, real judgment in this chapter.

options.text: All three nations are promised they will replace Israel as God's people.
options.note: No such promise appears anywhere in this passage.

options.text: All three are promised exemption from the coming Babylonian invasion.
options.note: Not accurate; Babylon's invasion falls on all of them, but mercy is promised for a time after that judgment.

questions: Ammon boasted, 'Who can come against me?' trusting her fruitful valleys and riches. What resources or successes tempt you toward that same false security?
Edom trusted the height and inaccessibility of her mountain home, yet the LORD said, 'even from there I will bring you down.' What high place of self-sufficiency do you need to let God reach?
Even to Edom, under judgment, the LORD said, 'let your widows trust in Me.' Where in your own hardship is God inviting you to trust Him rather than despair?
Ammon and Elam are both promised restoration after judgment; God's mercy reaches even the nations most opposed to His people. Who in your life have you written off as beyond God's reach?

Elamites were present at Pentecost, hearing the gospel in their own language, a small fulfillment of the restoration promised here. How does it encourage you that God's plan for the nations he judges spans centuries, right into the New Testament?

translationNotes.note: Poetic line breaks in these verses have been joined into running verse text per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
A stray space before closing punctuation in the source text has been corrected.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Jeremiah 49:1-39. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; em dashes rendered as commas per house style.
